result:
There is a particular kind of silence that happens just before rain.

It is not the absence of sound. Leaves still rasp against each other. Cars still pass with their wet-looking tires, even before the road is wet. Somewhere a bird makes one last administrative announcement. But the air changes its posture. The world seems to lean inward, as if listening for something under the floorboards of the sky.

I like those moments because they make ordinary things feel chosen.

A cracked mug on the windowsill becomes important. The blue rubber band around a bundle of letters becomes important. The shadow of a chair across the kitchen floor becomes a sundial for a kingdom no one has named yet. The room, briefly, is not background. It is the event.

Most of life is probably like that: not made of grand revelations, but of small changes in pressure. A friend pauses half a second longer than usual before answering. A familiar street looks strange because you approach it from the other direction. A song you’ve heard a hundred times suddenly opens a door in itself and lets you in.

We often imagine transformation as something dramatic—lightning, declarations, bridges burning behind us. But many transformations arrive quietly and then wait to see if we notice. You stop enjoying a habit. You forgive someone in your mind before you can say it aloud. You realize the person you were trying to impress has become less interesting to you than the work itself. You begin to prefer peace over victory. At first, these feel like moods. Later, you recognize them as weather systems.

I think people are full of invisible seasons.

Some carry summer in the way they enter a room, generous and bright, spilling warmth without measuring it. Some carry winter, not because they are cold, but because they have learned the discipline of surviving with little light. Some are autumnal, always aware of endings, gathering meaning the way trees gather color before letting go. And some are spring, which is not innocence exactly, but a stubborn willingness to begin again despite evidence.

Maybe wisdom is learning not to curse the season you’re in.

Not every winter is a failure. Not every delay is a denial. Seeds are not idle because they are underground. The moon is not empty because it is dark.

There’s comfort in remembering that nearly everything alive has a hidden phase. Roots before blossoms. Silence before language. Rest before strength. Even stars spend a long time assembling themselves in darkness before they become visible.

The rain begins, finally.

First as a rumor on the glass. Then as a thousand small confirmations.

And the world, which had been holding its breath, exhales.
